title: Contrast enhanced imaging through weakly scattering media with spatially entangled photons
authors: James Hubble, Rojan Abolhassani, Alessio D'Errico, Nazanin Dehghan, Yishai Klein, Yingwen Zhang, Ebrahim Karimi
abstract: Improving the image contrast of objects immersed in weakly scattering media can be achieved using various strategies. One common approach is to reject events associated with scattered photons in favor of the detection of ballistic photons. While this is traditionally done via time gating or spatial filtering, we propose a different approach based on probing the object with spatio-temporally entangled photon pairs. We show that coincidence detection, followed by post-selection on spatially correlated events, allows us to isolate ballistic from scattered bi-photons, thereby enhancing image contrast relative to a single-photon detection strategy, and simultaneously removes events due to background light. Our predictions are obtained via numerical simulations and confirmed by experiments conducted in two configurations where either both photons or only one illuminates the scene. In both scenarios, correlation post-selection shows an improvement in image contrast at the expense of higher shot noise due to the lower number of events. The latter can be partially compensated for by appropriately combining events from several post-selection windows. Our findings will enable extending imaging through scattering media into the quantum imaging framework in settings where adaptive optics, time gating, and spatial filtering are impractical.
summary: This paper proposes using spatially entangled photon pairs to achieve contrast-enhanced imaging through weakly scattering media. By employing coincidence detection and post-selection on correlated photon pairs, the technique effectively filters out scattered light, significantly improving image quality over standard single-photon measurements. This quantum approach offers a powerful tool for imaging in complex, scattering environments where traditional optical methods fail.
main_problem: The primary goal is to enhance the image contrast when imaging objects immersed in weakly scattering media.
main_result: Using coincidence detection and post-selection on spatially entangled photon pairs significantly enhances image contrast compared to single-photon or non-post-selected coincidence measurements.
method: The method involves exploiting the spatial entanglement of photon pairs and performing coincidence post-selection to filter out scattered bi-photons, thereby isolating the ballistic signal.
model_or_system: The system uses Spontaneous Parametric Down-Conversion (SPDC) to generate spatially entangled photon pairs which are then passed through a scattering medium and an object. The theoretical framework models the bi-photon wavefunction and the effect of random phase masks.
key_observables: Image contrast (quantified via Modulation Transfer Function, MTF), coincidence counts, and the ratio of post-selected coincidences to single-photon counts.
important_parameters: Scattering strength, quantified by the ratio $w_q/w_A$, and the degree of input spatial entanglement ($w_q/w_0$).
main_assumptions: The analysis relies on the double-Gaussian approximation for the bi-photon wavefunction and assumes that post-selection is effective even when time gating or spatial filtering is impractical.
figures_summary: Figures compare MTF and reconstructed images for single-photon, non-post-selected coincidence, and post-selected coincidence measurements, demonstrating contrast enhancement across different scattering regimes.
paper_structure: The paper progresses from conceptual schemes comparing classical vs. quantum filtering, through numerical simulations detailing the MTF dependence on scattering strength, and culminates in experimental demonstrations confirming the contrast improvement.
why_interesting: This work provides a quantum-enhanced imaging protocol that circumvents limitations of classical optical techniques (like time/spatial gating) in highly scattering environments, which is a key area of quantum optics research.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Conceptual scheme. a, In weakly scattering media (thick- ness d no longer than ∼5 times the mean free path ls) is possible to identify ballistic photons and isolate them from scattering events either using time gating or spatial filtering. Ballistic photons carry information on objects hidden behind or inside the scattering medium. b, In this work, we propose exploiting the fact that ballistic photon pairs preserve transverse position entanglement, and, thus, can be isolated from scattered biphotons via correlation post-selection.

Figure 2. Numerical simulations. a, Scheme of the simulated setup: spatially entangled photon pairs (EPS) are propagated in free-space to a random phase screen (S1) placed in the far field of the source. Then the pair is incident on an object (O), with spatially varying transmissivity, placed in the source image plane. A second random phase screen (S2) is placed in the object’s far field. Coincidence imaging (CI) is performed on the source/object image plane. L indicates lenses performing optical Fourier transforms. Insets show the spatial correlations (for a single scattering realization) along the x-coordinate in different propagation planes. The red dashed rectangle indicates the...

Figure 3. Experimental setup and results. a, Outline of the experimental setup. BBO, β-barium borate crystal cut for Type I SPDC. SLM1 and SLM2 are liquid crystal spatial light modulators used to introduce scattering in S1 and S2 planes, respectively. O is the amplitude sample to be imaged. L, Lens; M, mirror; BS, beam splitter. Spatially resolved coincidences are collected by a time-stamping camera, Tpx3Cam. b, Near field correlations after scattering. Coincidence postselection is obtained by selecting events where signal and idler coordinates are linearly correlated xi = xs + ξ. c, Image of the object immersed in a dynamic scattering medium as reconstructed by singles (g(1)), coincidences...

Figure 4. Correlation imaging with dynamic scattering. a, Outline of the experimental setup for correlation imaging. BBO, β-barium bo- rate crystal cut for Type I SPDC. A beam splitter (BS) in the far field separates signal and idler photons into an object arm and a reference arm. SLM1 and SLM2 are liquid crystal spatial light modulators used to introduce scattering. O is the amplitude sample to be imaged. L, Lens; M, mirror; BS, beam splitter. Spatially resolved coincidences are collected by a time-stamping camera, Tpx3Cam. b, Image of the object immersed in a dynamic scattering medium as reconstructed by singles (g(1)), coincidences (Γs), and post-selected coincidences (Γ(n=3) post ). RMS...

Figure 5. Retrieving image details from correlation postselection. Postselection of correlation data allows to recover object details that would otherwise be completely scrambled by the scattering medium. In this example, where the object is an insect wing, it is seen how scattering hides the venation pattern. Some of the veins are, however, slightly visible when looking at the postselection data. This is more evident when digital filtering is applied to the recovered images. The corresponding images in the absence of scattering (before and after digital filtering) are shown on the left for comparison. These data were acquired in the single-photon-scattering configuration.
